Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Training-Free Layer-Wise Quantization via Spectral Stability Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: Existing mixed-precision inference strategies rely on heuristic layer sensitivity metrics (e.g., activation magnitude, gradient norm) that conflate orthogonal sources of numerical instability. We hypothesize that explicit spectral curvature diagnostics—specifically, local Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance decay—can serve as deterministic, training-free proxies for precision requirements, enabling provably stable layer-wise quantization routing. This direction is essential because: (1) current methods lack formal stability guarantees under precision switching, (2) layer sensitivity and quantization robustness are distinct properties requiring separate geometric characterization, and (3) a curvature-grounded framework can yield PAC-Bayes generalization bounds that directly constrain quantization error propagation, unifying theoretical guarantees with practical routing decisions.
Related Work: Recent work on test-time compute scaling (e.g., Spectral Latent Steering) demonstrates the power of training-free spectral diagnostics for adaptive inference. However, these methods focus on subspace steering for reasoning, not precision allocation. Orthogonal work on mixed-precision quantization (e.g., BRECQ, OCS) uses layer-wise sensitivity metrics based on activation statistics or gradient magnitudes, but these are agnostic to local loss landscape curvature—a critical determinant of quantization robustness. Layer-wise Hessian analysis has been explored in pruning and compression, but never in the context of deterministic precision routing with formal stability bounds. Our proposal distinctly combines: (1) training-free Hessian estimation via power iteration on gradient covariance, (2) explicit condition number thresholds for precision tier assignment, and (3) PAC-Bayes verification of quantization error bounds under routing decisions. This is not a trivial extension: we replace heuristic sensitivity with rigorous curvature geometry, yielding falsifiable predictions about which layers tolerate aggressive quantization.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization can dramatically reduce inference latency, but current routing strategies rely on opaque layer sensitivity heuristics that provide no formal stability guarantees. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that assigns layer-wise precision tiers by directly analyzing local loss landscape geometry. The core insight is that a layer's tolerance for quantization is determined not by activation magnitude but by Hessian conditioning—layers with well-conditioned Hessians tolerate aggressive quantization with bounded error propagation. CAPR performs three deterministic, pre-execution diagnostics: (1) block-wise Hessian conditioning estimation via power iteration on gradient covariance matrices, (2) spectral gap analysis to quantify feature entanglement, and (3) PAC-Bayes error bound computation that formally constrains allowable quantization noise per layer. Precision tiers are assigned via a curvature-aware routing table: layers exceeding a condition number threshold receive higher precision, while well-conditioned layers are aggressively quantized. We validate CAPR on LLaMs and ViTs using single-GPU inference, comparing against magnitude-based and gradient-based routing. Experiments measure: (1) accuracy preservation under different quantization schemes, (2) empirical alignment between predicted condition numbers and observed quantization sensitivity, (3) amortized diagnostic overhead (< 2% compute), and (4) confidence intervals across multiple random seeds. CAPR yields a theoretically grounded, numerically transparent alternative to heuristic precision allocation, with explicit stability radii and reproducible routing decisions.

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Hessian Conditioning Estimation and Validation
Experiments.description: For each layer in LLaM-7B and ViT-B/32, estimate block-wise Hessian condition numbers using power iteration on gradient covariance matrices computed over a small calibration set (100–500 forward passes). Specifically: (1) sample random mini-batches from calibration data, (2) compute per-layer gradient covariance via G_l = E[∇_l L ∇_l L^T], (3) estimate κ(H_l) ≈ λ_max(G_l) / λ_min(G_l) using 5–10 power iterations, (4) validate estimates against finite-difference Hessian-vector products on a held-out subset. Metric: correlation between estimated and ground-truth condition numbers (target: ρ > 0.85), and wall-clock overhead for full diagnostic pass (target: < 2% of baseline inference).
Experiments.algorithmic_details: Power iteration for λ_max: v_{k+1} = G_l v_k / ||G_l v_k||_2, repeat 10 iterations. Estimate λ_min via inverse power iteration on G_l after Lanczos preconditioning. Use block-level covariance (e.g., per attention head or per residual block) to avoid O(d^3) overhead.

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: Precision Tier Assignment via Curvature Thresholds
Experiments.description: Define a curvature-aware routing table: layers with κ(H_l) < 10 → INT8, 10 ≤ κ(H_l) < 50 → INT8 with FP16 residuals, κ(H_l) ≥ 50 → FP16. For each model (LLaM-7B, LLaM-13B, ViT-B/32), apply CAPR routing and compare against: (1) magnitude-based routing (BRECQ-style), (2) gradient norm-based routing, (3) uniform INT8 quantization. Metrics: (a) end-task accuracy (MMLU, GSM8K for LLaMs; ImageNet for ViT), (b) inference latency, (c) memory footprint. Report mean ± std over 5 random seeds with different calibration data splits.
Experiments.algorithmic_details: Routing decision: for layer l, compute κ_l via power iteration. Assign precision tier p_l based on κ_l thresholds. Quantize activations and weights in layer l using tier p_l (e.g., symmetric affine quantization for INT8). Use per-channel quantization for weights, per-token for activations.

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: PAC-Bayes Generalization Bounds on Quantization Error
Experiments.description: For each layer, derive a PAC-Bayes bound on the quantization error propagation under the assigned precision tier. Specifically: (1) compute the spectral norm of the Jacobian J_l (layer input-to-output mapping), (2) estimate the quantization noise magnitude Δ_l as a function of precision (e.g., Δ_l ≈ 2^{-b_l} for b_l bits), (3) apply PAC-Bayes to bound the expected loss increase: E[L_quantized] ≤ E[L_original] + λ Σ_l ||J_l||_2 Δ_l + O(1/√n). Validate bounds empirically: measure actual loss increase under quantization and compare against predicted bound. Metric: tightness ratio (actual loss increase / predicted bound), target < 2.0.
Experiments.algorithmic_details: Jacobian spectral norm: ||J_l||_2 ≈ largest singular value of per-layer weight matrix (via power iteration). Quantization noise: Δ_l = (max_activation - min_activation) / 2^{b_l} for b_l-bit quantization. PAC-Bayes bound: use Catoni's inequality with union bound over layers.

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Feature Entanglement Diagnostics
Experiments.description: Compute spectral gap analysis on the gradient covariance matrix G_l to quantify feature entanglement per layer. Define entanglement metric as gap ratio: e_l = λ_2(G_l) / λ_1(G_l) (higher gap = lower entanglement = more quantizable). Correlate entanglement metrics with empirical quantization sensitivity: for each layer, measure accuracy drop under INT8 quantization and compare against predicted entanglement. Hypothesis: layers with low entanglement (high spectral gap) tolerate aggressive quantization. Metric: Spearman correlation between entanglement metric and quantization sensitivity (target: ρ > 0.75).
Experiments.algorithmic_details: Spectral gap: compute top-2 eigenvalues of G_l via Lanczos. Entanglement = λ_2 / λ_1. Correlate with layer-wise quantization error (e.g., measured via layer-wise MSE before/after quantization).

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Ablation Study on Routing Components
Experiments.description: Isolate the contribution of each diagnostic: (1) CAPR-full (all three: conditioning + entanglement + PAC-Bayes bounds), (2) CAPR-cond-only (condition number only), (3) CAPR-gap-only (spectral gap only), (4) magnitude-baseline (standard heuristic). Measure accuracy, latency, and memory for each variant on LLaM-7B and ViT-B/32. Metric: accuracy drop relative to FP32, inference latency, amortized diagnostic overhead. Report 95% confidence intervals.
Experiments.algorithmic_details: Each variant uses the same quantization scheme (symmetric INT8/FP16) but differs in routing decision. CAPR-full uses all three diagnostics to set precision tiers; CAPR-cond-only uses only κ(H_l); etc.

Experiments.name: Experiment 6: Transferability and Generalization
Experiments.description: Test whether CAPR routing decisions generalize across different tasks and data distributions. Train routing table on one dataset (e.g., WikiText for LLaMs), then apply to a held-out task (e.g., MMLU, GSM8K). Hypothesis: curvature-based routing is more transferable than magnitude-based routing because it captures intrinsic layer properties rather than data-dependent statistics. Metric: accuracy drop when transferring routing decisions across tasks (target: < 1% additional drop vs. task-specific routing).
Experiments.algorithmic_details: Compute condition numbers on calibration set from source task. Apply same precision tiers to target task. Measure accuracy and compare against task-specific CAPR routing.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Hessian Estimation Noise: Power iteration estimates of condition numbers may be noisy on small calibration sets. Mitigation: use multiple random seeds, Lanczos preconditioning, and explicit confidence interval reporting.

Threshold Sensitivity: Precision tier thresholds (κ < 10, etc.) are heuristic and may require tuning per architecture. Mitigation: perform systematic sensitivity analysis and report threshold ranges where CAPR outperforms baselines.

Gradient Covariance Rank Deficiency: In layers with low intrinsic dimensionality, gradient covariance matrices may be rank-deficient, violating conditioning number assumptions. Mitigation: add explicit rank diagnostics and handle low-rank cases via pseudo-inverse or regularization.

Limited Scalability to Very Large Models: Covariance computation scales as O(d^2) in layer dimension; for very wide layers (e.g., MLP in LLaMs), this may exceed memory budgets. Mitigation: use block-level covariance (per-head, per-residual block) to reduce dimensionality, and report memory overhead explicitly.

Transferability Uncertainty: Curvature-based routing may be sensitive to batch normalization, layer normalization, or other architectural variations. Mitigation: test on multiple architectures (LLaM, ViT, MLP-Mixer) and report per-architecture routing tables.

PAC-Bayes Bound Looseness: Derived bounds may be loose in practice, limiting their utility for routing. Mitigation: empirically validate tightness and compare against empirical loss increase; use empirical Bernstein bounds if available.

Computational Overhead: Even with optimizations, diagnostic overhead may exceed 2% budget on some hardware. Mitigation: implement on single GPU, profile carefully, and report exact wall-clock times with confidence intervals.
